@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/applied behavior analysis / positive behavior support / skill replacement / proactive teaching / developmental intervention / parent training/replacement behavior teaching / functional communication / proactive skill building / skill deficits vs. performance deficits / teaching alternatives/9-materials-that-help-teaching-what-to-do#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does i spent the first five years of my daughter's life telling her what not to do. don't grab toys. don't interrupt. don't run inside. don't whine. don't touch. don't, don't, don't. and i was exhausted. nothing seemed to work for long. she'd stop one problem behavior and start another. it felt like whack-a-mole — i'd address one issue and two more would pop up. her occupational therapist finally helped me understand: my daughter wasn't being defiant. she had skill deficits. she grabbed toys because she didn't know how to ask for a turn. she interrupted because she didn't know how to wait. she ran because she didn't know how to get her energy out appropriately. she whined because she didn't have words for her feelings. every 'don't' i said assumed she already knew the alternative. but she didn't. you can't just remove a behavior — you have to replace it with something better. now i teach her what to do, and the problem behaviors are slowly fading because she has new tools?

acceptedAnswer.text: REPLACEMENT BEHAVIOR TEACHING / FUNCTIONAL COMMUNICATION TRAINING / DIFFERENTIAL REINFORCEMENT / PROACTIVE SKILL BUILDING / TEACHING FUNCTIONAL ALTERNATIVES! Teaching what TO do refers to the proactive approach of explicitly teaching children alternative behaviors that serve the same function as their problematic behaviors but in socially appropriate ways. This is grounded in the behavioral principle that all behavior serves a function (getting something wanted, escaping something unwanted, sensory input, or attention). Simply suppressing a behavior without teaching an alternative creates a behavioral vacuum — the child still has the same need but no appropriate way to meet it. Effective behavior intervention focuses on functional replacement: identifying what the child is trying to achieve and teaching an appropriate behavior that achieves the same outcome. Teaching alternatives is more effective than punishment because it builds skills rather than just suppressing behavior, and it leads to lasting change rather than temporary suppression.

mainEntity.name: What is Replacement Behavior Teaching / Functional Communication Training / Differential Reinforcement / Proactive Skill Building / Teaching Functional Alternatives?

acceptedAnswer.text: Teaching what TO do refers to the proactive approach of explicitly teaching children alternative behaviors that serve the same function as their problematic behaviors but in socially appropriate ways. This is grounded in the behavioral principle that all behavior serves a function (getting something wanted, escaping something unwanted, sensory input, or attention). Simply suppressing a behavior without teaching an alternative creates a behavioral vacuum — the child still has the same need but no appropriate way to meet it. Effective behavior intervention focuses on functional replacement: identifying what the child is trying to achieve and teaching an appropriate behavior that achieves the same outcome. Teaching alternatives is more effective than punishment because it builds skills rather than just suppressing behavior, and it leads to lasting change rather than temporary suppression. Common signs include: Child seems to know what NOT to do but does it anyway, Behavior problems decrease temporarily then return, Child stops one problem behavior but starts another, Child appears to lack skills, not just motivation, Same need is expressed through different problem behaviors.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual 'Instead Of' Cards and Choice Menus help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Visual 'Instead Of' cards make alternatives concrete and visible. Instead of abstractly telling a child 'use your words,' the card shows exactly what to do: 'Instead of hitting, I can ask for space.' 'Instead of grabbing, I can say may I have a turn.' These cards transform vague expectations into specific, pictured alternatives. Choice menus extend this concept by offering multiple appropriate options: 'When I'm frustrated, I can: take deep breaths, squeeze a stress ball, ask for help, or take a break.' Having visible alternatives reduces cognitive load in the moment — the child doesn't have to remember or generate alternatives when upset, they just need to look at the card and choose. Price: ₹100–400. DIY option: Create Instead Of cards for your child's most common challenging behaviors. Format: Left side shows the problematic behavior (crossed out or with red border), right side shows 1-3 alternatives (with green checkmark). Use real photos of your child when possible. Include simple words and clear pictures. Laminate for durability. Create choice menus for common situations: 'When I'm angry, I can...' 'When I want something, I can...' 'When I need a break, I can...' Post in relevant locations. Teach during calm times. Point to cards in the moment. Praise when child uses alternatives. Safety: Ensure alternatives are developmentally appropriate and actually achievable for your child.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Story Scripts for Replacement Behaviors help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social stories teach replacement behaviors within the context of a narrative. Rather than just presenting a rule ('ask nicely'), social stories explain why, show what it looks like, and describe how it feels. A social story about asking for help might say: 'Sometimes I need help. That's okay. Instead of crying, I can say 'help please.' When I ask nicely, people want to help me. Asking makes me feel proud.' Stories work because they provide context, model appropriate self-talk, and normalize the learning process. Children hear the replacement behavior within a story they can relate to, which makes it more memorable and meaningful than a simple rule. Price: ₹0–500. DIY option: Write social stories for behaviors your child is learning. Structure: (1) Describe the situation, (2) Acknowledge the feeling or need, (3) Introduce the replacement behavior, (4) Describe positive outcomes, (5) Normalize the learning. Use first-person perspective ('I'). Include photos of your child demonstrating the replacement behavior. Keep stories short (5-10 sentences for young children). Read daily during calm times. Refer to story in the moment: 'Remember your story about asking for help?' Create stories for: asking vs. grabbing, waiting vs. interrupting, taking breaks vs. melting down, using words vs. hitting. Safety: Stories should be positive and solution-focused, not shaming about problem behaviors.

mainEntity.name: How does a Role-Play Props and Practice Scenarios help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Replacement behaviors must be practiced to become automatic. Role-play props and practice scenarios create safe opportunities to rehearse alternatives before they're needed in real situations. Using puppets, dolls, figurines, or even stuffed animals, children can practice 'what do we do when we want a toy someone else has?' or 'what do we do when we feel angry?' Practice during calm moments builds the motor patterns and language needed to use alternatives under stress. Props make practice engaging and reduce the pressure of practicing 'on yourself.' A child might readily show a puppet how to ask nicely even if they struggle to do it themselves — and the practice still builds the skill. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Gather props for role-play: puppets, dolls, figurines, stuffed animals, or simple sock puppets. Create common scenarios to practice: 'Tommy wants the toy Sally has. What can Tommy do?' 'This puppet feels frustrated. What can she do?' Model the replacement behavior first, then have child practice with props. Praise practice attempts. Gradually increase difficulty: start with simple scenarios, add emotions, add social complexity. Practice regularly, not just after problems. Connect to real situations: 'Remember how your puppet asked nicely? You can do that too!' Make it fun — dramatic voices, silly scenarios, child's favorite characters. Safety: Keep practice positive and low-pressure. This is rehearsal, not testing.

mainEntity.name: How does a Communication Cards and AAC Supports help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many challenging behaviors occur because children can't communicate their needs effectively. Communication cards and AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) supports give children immediate access to replacement behaviors that express needs without problem behaviors. A child who hits when overwhelmed can instead hand over a 'break please' card. A child who screams when hungry can point to a 'snack' picture. Communication cards work because they lower the barrier to appropriate communication — the words are right there, ready to use. For children with limited verbal language, AAC is the replacement behavior. For children with emerging language, cards provide backup when words fail under stress. Price: ₹100–500 (or free apps). DIY option: Create core communication cards for: help, break, more, all done, wait, my turn, angry, sad, scared, hungry, thirsty. Use clear pictures with words. Laminate for durability. Keep accessible at all times — on a ring, in pocket, on keychain, posted in rooms. Teach during calm moments: show card, say word, practice exchange. Honor all card use immediately at first (to reinforce the communication, not the problem behavior). Expand vocabulary as child learns. Consider AAC apps for tablet (many free options). Cards should travel with child to all settings. Teach all caregivers to respond to cards. Safety: Always honor communication attempts, especially early in teaching. The goal is to make communication more effective than problem behavior.

mainEntity.name: How does a Self-Regulation Toolkits and Calm-Down Kits help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Self-regulation is a replacement for emotional meltdowns, aggression, and other behaviors that stem from dysregulation. Self-regulation toolkits give children concrete tools to use instead of losing control. A calm-down kit might include: a stress ball, breathing cards, a pinwheel for deep breaths, headphones for noise reduction, a weighted lap pad, a favorite fidget, and visual coping steps. The kit provides tangible alternatives: 'Instead of screaming, I can squeeze my stress ball.' 'Instead of running away, I can put on my headphones.' Having tools ready and portable means children can access replacement behaviors immediately, rather than trying to remember abstract strategies while dysregulated. Price: ₹300–1000. DIY option: Create a personalized calm-down kit based on what works for your child. Include: tactile items (stress balls, fidgets, putty), breathing tools (pinwheel, bubbles, breathing cards), sensory items (noise-canceling headphones, sunglasses, weighted item), visual supports (calm-down steps, feeling cards), comfort items (small stuffed animal, family photo). Store in a special box or bag. Keep in consistent, accessible location. Create portable version for school and outings. Teach each tool during calm moments. Practice regularly. Point to kit when child is escalating: 'Get your calm-down kit!' Praise all attempts to use tools. Safety: All items should be safe for unsupervised use. Supervise weighted items with young children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Behavior Rehearsal Video Models help with replacement behavior teaching?

acceptedAnswer.text: Video models show replacement behaviors in action. Children watch videos of themselves or peers demonstrating appropriate alternatives, which provides a powerful template for their own behavior. Video is effective because it captures the whole sequence: the situation, the choice point, the replacement behavior, and the positive outcome. Children can watch videos repeatedly, pausing to discuss key moments. Self-modeling (watching videos of themselves successfully using alternatives) is particularly powerful — it shows children they can do it because they've already done it. Video models work especially well for children who are visual learners or who struggle with verbal instruction. Price: ₹0–300 (smartphone recording). DIY option: Create short video models (30-90 seconds) for target replacement behaviors. Options: (1) Film your child successfully using the alternative (catch them doing it right), (2) Film siblings or peers modeling the behavior, (3) Use parent as model, (4) Create simple animation or puppet videos. Structure: show situation → show choice point → show replacement behavior → show positive outcome. Keep videos simple and focused on one skill. Create library of videos for different situations. Watch together during calm times, discussing what the person in the video did. Watch before situations where skill is needed. Consider video self-modeling: editing together clips of child's successful moments. Safety: Keep videos private. Only use peers with permission from their parents.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Replacement Behavior Teaching
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Teaching replacement behaviors is the most effective, most ethical, and most sustainable approach to behavior change. This approach is rooted in the understanding that all behavior serves a function — behavior happens because it works to get something (attention, items, sensory input) or escape something (demands, situations, sensory input). Simply suppressing a behavior without providing an alternative leaves the child with an unmet need and no appropriate way to meet it. This creates a 'behavioral vacuum' that will inevitably be filled by another problem behavior, often one that's worse. The solution is functional replacement: identifying what the child is trying to achieve and teaching an appropriate behavior that achieves the same outcome more effectively.

item.description: Many challenging behaviors occur because children can't communicate their needs effectively. Communication cards and AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) supports give children immediate access to replacement behaviors that express needs without problem behaviors. A child who hits when overwhelmed can instead hand over a 'break please' card. A child who screams when hungry can point to a 'snack' picture. Communication cards work because they lower the barrier to appropriate communication — the words are right there, ready to use. For children with limited verbal language, AAC is the replacement behavior. For children with emerging language, cards provide backup when words fail under stress.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create core communication cards for: help, break, more, all done, wait, my turn, angry, sad, scared, hungry, thirsty. Use clear pictures with words. Laminate for durability. Keep accessible at all times — on a ring, in pocket, on keychain, posted in rooms. Teach during calm moments: show card, say word, practice exchange. Honor all card use immediately at first (to reinforce the communication, not the problem behavior). Expand vocabulary as child learns. Consider AAC apps for tablet (many free options). Cards should travel with child to all settings. Teach all caregivers to respond to cards.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always honor communication attempts, especially early in teaching. The goal is to make communication more effective than problem behavior.

item.name: Self-Regulation Toolkits and Calm-Down Kits
item.description: Self-regulation is a replacement for emotional meltdowns, aggression, and other behaviors that stem from dysregulation. Self-regulation toolkits give children concrete tools to use instead of losing control. A calm-down kit might include: a stress ball, breathing cards, a pinwheel for deep breaths, headphones for noise reduction, a weighted lap pad, a favorite fidget, and visual coping steps. The kit provides tangible alternatives: 'Instead of screaming, I can squeeze my stress ball.' 'Instead of running away, I can put on my headphones.' Having tools ready and portable means children can access replacement behaviors immediately, rather than trying to remember abstract strategies while dysregulated.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a personalized calm-down kit based on what works for your child. Include: tactile items (stress balls, fidgets, putty), breathing tools (pinwheel, bubbles, breathing cards), sensory items (noise-canceling headphones, sunglasses, weighted item), visual supports (calm-down steps, feeling cards), comfort items (small stuffed animal, family photo). Store in a special box or bag. Keep in consistent, accessible location. Create portable version for school and outings. Teach each tool during calm moments. Practice regularly. Point to kit when child is escalating: 'Get your calm-down kit!' Praise all attempts to use tools.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: All items should be safe for unsupervised use. Supervise weighted items with young children.

item.name: Behavior Rehearsal Video Models
item.description: Video models show replacement behaviors in action. Children watch videos of themselves or peers demonstrating appropriate alternatives, which provides a powerful template for their own behavior. Video is effective because it captures the whole sequence: the situation, the choice point, the replacement behavior, and the positive outcome. Children can watch videos repeatedly, pausing to discuss key moments. Self-modeling (watching videos of themselves successfully using alternatives) is particularly powerful — it shows children they can do it because they've already done it. Video models work especially well for children who are visual learners or who struggle with verbal instruction.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create short video models (30-90 seconds) for target replacement behaviors. Options: (1) Film your child successfully using the alternative (catch them doing it right), (2) Film siblings or peers modeling the behavior, (3) Use parent as model, (4) Create simple animation or puppet videos. Structure: show situation → show choice point → show replacement behavior → show positive outcome. Keep videos simple and focused on one skill. Create library of videos for different situations. Watch together during calm times, discussing what the person in the video did. Watch before situations where skill is needed. Consider video self-modeling: editing together clips of child's successful moments.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep videos private. Only use peers with permission from their parents.

item.name: Problem-Solving Visual Frameworks
item.description: Problem-solving is the ultimate replacement behavior — teaching children to work through challenges rather than reacting with problematic behaviors. Visual problem-solving frameworks break down the steps so children can follow them independently. A simple framework might be: (1) What's the problem? (2) How do I feel? (3) What can I do? (4) What happens if I do that? (5) What will I try? Posted visual frameworks become external guides that children reference when stuck. Over time, the steps become internalized. Problem-solving frameworks replace impulsive reactions with thoughtful responses — instead of hitting when a toy is taken, the child works through the steps to find a solution.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a visual problem-solving framework appropriate for your child's age. Simple version (ages 3-5): 'Stop → Think → Do' with pictures. Intermediate (ages 5-8): 'Problem → Feeling → Options → Choose → Try' with icons. Advanced (ages 8+): 'Identify problem → Brainstorm solutions → Evaluate options → Choose best → Try → Evaluate outcome.' Post framework in visible locations. Practice with hypothetical scenarios first. Use during real situations by pointing to steps: 'What's the problem? Let's look at your chart.' Teach solutions for common problems. Celebrate problem-solving attempts even if solutions aren't perfect.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Framework complexity should match child's cognitive and language level.

item.name: Positive Behavior Prompt Cards and Cue Systems
item.description: Prompt cards and cue systems remind children of replacement behaviors before problems occur. Rather than waiting for a problem and then correcting, prompts proactively cue the alternative. A prompt card before entering a store might show: 'Walking feet, quiet voice, hands to myself.' A visual cue before a sibling comes over might remind: 'I can ask for a turn. I can play together. I can find my own toy.' These preemptive prompts set children up for success by activating the replacement behavior before the challenging situation. The goal is to prevent problems, not just respond to them. Prompt cards work especially well at transition points and before known difficult situations.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create prompt cards for challenging situations: entering stores, visitors arriving, going to restaurants, transitions, waiting rooms, car rides. Each card shows 2-4 expected behaviors with pictures. Review card BEFORE entering situation, not during. Have child say the expectations aloud. Keep cards on a ring or in car for easy access. Create routine of reviewing before known challenges. Praise following the prompts: 'You remembered walking feet, just like your card showed!' Update cards as child masters behaviors. Fade prompts gradually as behaviors become automatic.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep expectations realistic. 2-4 behaviors is enough; too many overwhelm.

item.name: Reinforcement Systems for Alternative Behaviors
item.description: Replacement behaviors only stick if they're more rewarding than the problem behaviors they replace. Reinforcement systems ensure that appropriate alternatives receive immediate, consistent positive consequences. A token system might specifically target replacement behaviors: 'You asked nicely — that's a token!' 'You used your calm-down kit — that's two tokens!' By reinforcing alternatives heavily, they become more attractive than the old behaviors. The key is that replacement behaviors need to work at least as well as problem behaviors — and reinforcement systems make sure they do. Over time, natural consequences and intrinsic satisfaction take over, but initially, systematic reinforcement is crucial to establish the new behavior pattern.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a reinforcement system specifically targeting replacement behaviors. List 3-5 replacement behaviors to reinforce (e.g., asking nicely, using calm-down tools, waiting, taking turns, using words instead of hitting). Provide immediate reinforcement when these alternatives are used: verbal praise, tokens, small rewards. Make the reinforcement more powerful than what problem behavior got. Be consistent — reinforce alternatives every time at first. Catch them being good — actively look for opportunities to reinforce. Track data on alternative behavior use. Fade to intermittent reinforcement as behaviors strengthen. Eventually transition to natural reinforcement (the alternative works, and that's enough).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Don't inadvertently reinforce problem behaviors by giving attention or desired outcomes after them.

socialMedia.caption: Stop saying 'don't.' Start teaching 'do this instead.' Children can't stop a behavior unless they have something to do instead. Here are 9 materials that help teach what TO do: Instead Of cards, social stories, role-play props, communication cards, calm-down kits, video models, problem-solving frameworks, prompt cards, and reinforcement systems. Teach the replacement. Watch the problem fade. 🌱 #TeachingAlternatives #ReplacementBehaviors #PinnacleBlooms
